content:
The idea of assigning numbers to uncertain outcomes grew from **16th–17th century** work on games of chance (**Pascal**, **Fermat**, **Huygens**), where winnings were computed from probabilistic events.

**Bernoulli** and later **De Moivre** formalized discrete probability over finite outcomes—the ancestor of today's PMFs.

In the 18th–19th centuries, **Laplace** and **Gauss** developed continuous models (normal distribution) for measurement error and natural variation, establishing PDFs as central objects.

**Kolmogorov's axioms** (1933) placed probability on a rigorous measure-theoretic foundation: random variables became measurable functions, and CDFs unified discrete and continuous cases.

With statistics and computing, empirical CDFs, histograms, and kernel density estimates bridged theory to data.

In modern ML and Gen AI, random variables appear everywhere: labels as discrete RVs, latent vectors as continuous RVs, and generative models as learned high-dimensional distributions—extending classical PMF/PDF/CDF ideas to billion-parameter implicit densities.

timeline.label: Classical probability
timeline.description: Pascal, Fermat and Bernoulli analyze games of chance with discrete equally likely outcomes.

timeline.label: Continuous distributions
timeline.description: Laplace and Gauss develop PDF-based models for errors and natural variation.

timeline.label: Kolmogorov axioms
timeline.description: Measure-theoretic foundation defines random variables and unifies CDFs for all types.

timeline.label: ML and generative AI
timeline.description: Softmax PMFs, Gaussian PDFs, and learned generative densities operationalize random variables in models at scale.
